title: Zimtsterne
subtitle: German cinnamon star cookies with white meringue glaze
excerpt: German Christmas cookies made almost entirely from ground almonds and egg whites — no flour, no butter. They are chewy and intensely spiced with cinnamon, set under a crisp white meringue glaze that coats the top of each star before baking. Naturally gluten-free.
sourceNotes: Zimtsterne (cinnamon stars) are one of the oldest documented German Christmas biscuits, with recipes appearing in German domestic cookbooks throughout the nineteenth century. The almond-and-egg-white composition, glazed with a meringue icing before baking, is consistent across all documented versions. Recipes appear in various nineteenth-century German baking manuals and in regional Advent baking traditions from Baden-Württemberg and Bavaria. The recipe here follows the established formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a single layer, then stack once solid with baking paper between layers. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improve over two to three days in a tin. Keep for up to three weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 130°C. The glaze must remain white — if the oven is too hot it yellows and the cookies toughen. They are done when the glaze is set and just dry to the touch, and the cookie is firm at the edges but still slightly soft in the centre.

glossaryTerms.definition: The white meringue-like glaze applied to the surface of each star before baking. It is made from lightly whisked egg white and icing sugar, beaten until thick and smooth but not quite stiff. Spread over the rolled dough before cutting, not after — cutting through the coated sheet produces cleaner stars than glazing individual cut-outs. The glaze sets to a crisp, opaque coat in the oven; if it turns yellow or cracks, the oven temperature was too high.

content.text: The technique that distinguishes zimtsterne from most other Christmas biscuits is the

: spread the glaze over the rolled sheet of dough before cutting, not after. Working on coated dough gives cleaner star shapes and avoids smearing the glaze during assembly. The dough itself is simply almond flour bound with egg white — no flour, no butter.

Whisk the two egg whites with the icing sugar, cinnamon, lemon zest, and salt until the mixture forms a thick, sticky dough — you may need to knead the last of the almond flour in by hand. The dough should hold together when pressed but not be wet. If it crumbles, add a few drops of water; if it sticks to your hands, dust with a little more almond flour. Form into a disc, wrap in cling film, and rest at room temperature for 1 hour.

Make the glaze: whisk the remaining egg white and icing sugar together until thick, smooth, and opaque. Heat the oven to 150°C conventional. Line baking trays with baking paper. Dust the work surface generously with icing sugar and roll the dough to 8 mm thickness. Spread the glaze evenly over the surface in a thin, even coat. Cut out stars with a 5 to 6 cm star cutter, dipping the cutter in icing sugar between each cut. Place on the lined trays.

Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the glaze is set and just dry to the touch, and the edges of each star feel firm while the centre remains slightly soft. The glaze should stay white. Cool completely on the trays before removing — they firm as they cool and are fragile while warm.
